claims.text: Uncanny Valley is the anxiety that arises when an artificial figure looks almost but not quite human.

evidence.quote: This anxiety is called Uncanny Valley
Uncanny is used to describe something that

claims.rationale: Definitional claim the entire video is built around. Aligns with the mainstream definition of the term in robotics and CGI literature.

claims.text: The term 'Uncanny Valley' was coined in 1970 by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori.

evidence.quote:
The term Uncanny Valley was first hypothesized
in 1970 by Japanese roboticist Masahiro
Mori

claims.rationale: Historical attribution matches widely documented record: Mori's 1970 essay 'Bukimi no Tani' in Energy is the well-established origin of the term.

claims.text: The animation in Polar Express landed squarely in Uncanny Valley, which is why the film failed commercially.

evidence.quote:
animation
style landed right in the
middle of Uncanny Valley

claims.rationale: The Polar Express / Uncanny Valley causation story is a popular-culture commonplace, but 'legendary bomb' overstates reality — the film grossed over $300M worldwide. The uncanny-valley criticism is real, the commercial-failure framing is not fully accurate.

claims.text: Test audiences of Shrek were traumatized by the originally lifelike Princess Fiona, so the studio redesigned her to look more cartoonish.

evidence.quote:
Princess Fiona character actually reduced
children to tears

to fix the Princess Fiona
problem, the studio actually went back and made
Shrek feel less lifelike

claims.rationale: The Shrek test-screening anecdote is widely repeated in animation lore (commonly traced to a DreamWorks story by Andrew Adamson). Omitting directTruth because the video does not cite a specific source and the anecdote has grown in the retelling.

dependencies.rationale: Concrete example of Uncanny Valley in commercial animation.

claims.text: Honda's ASIMO avoids triggering Uncanny Valley because its designers deliberately kept its appearance obviously robotic rather than human-looking.

evidence.quote:
Asimo is clearly a robot, a
simple machine and nothing more

claims.rationale: Honda's design choice to keep ASIMO's proportions astronaut-like rather than realistic human is publicly documented and cited widely in HRI literature as a deliberate uncanny-valley avoidance.

dependencies.rationale: Engineering example of successfully sidestepping the valley by staying on the pre-human-realism side of the curve.

claims.text: A robot that acts human but looks artificial is more agreeable than one that looks human but acts artificial.

evidence.quote: put a human head on the thing

claims.rationale: Consistent with Mori's original curve: appearance-realism and motion-realism interact, with motion-driven mismatches producing sharper aversion. The asymmetric framing (looks-vs-acts) is a plausible corollary supported by several follow-up studies.

dependencies.rationale: ASIMO exemplifies the design principle: human-like behavior, clearly-machine body.

claims.text: Mather and Reichling's study of 80 real-world robots found that participants trusted clearly-robotic robots more than human-like ones when asked to hand over money.

evidence.quote:
Maya Mather and David Reichling studied 80
real world robots

the robots that were
closest to human form close to the valley
threshold, they weren

claims.rationale: Mathur & Reichling (2016) 'Navigating a social world with android cohabitants' is a real Cognition paper with an 80-robot stimulus set finding a valley in both likability and trust. The video's gloss is loose but the core result checks out.

dependencies.rationale: Empirical evidence that the valley exists as a measurable effect.

claims.text: Uncanny Valley is triggered when we believe a robot can reason, learn, and feel like a human.

evidence.quote:
we jump
into Uncanny Valley when we believe a robot
can reason, learn and feel like humans do

claims.rationale: This is one candidate mechanism (the 'mind perception' account by Gray & Wegner). It competes with other accounts (categorical-boundary violation, evolutionary threat detection) the video itself also advances, so directTruth is pinned mid-range as a plausible but non-exclusive mechanism.

dependencies.rationale: Proposes a specific cognitive trigger for the phenomenon.

claims.text: Gray and Wegner's research showed robots are only unnerving when people attribute the capacity for experience to them.

evidence.quote:
Kurt Gray and Daniel Wegner found that robots
were only unnerving when people thought they
had the ability to sense and experience

claims.rationale: Gray & Wegner (2012) 'Feeling robots and human zombies' Cognition paper — an actual finding that the experience-perception dimension drives creepiness more than agency. The video's summary is accurate.

dependencies.rationale: Named empirical basis for the 'reason / learn / feel' trigger claim.

claims.text: Creating artificial life that crosses Uncanny Valley is beyond current engineering but is on the horizon.

evidence.quote:
Creating artificial life that makes the leap
over Uncanny Valley is beyond our current
state of engineering

claims.rationale: Speculative forecast. Since this video's filming, photorealistic avatars and generative-AI faces have narrowed the gap for static/short-form content, but fully lifelike embodied robots remain out of reach. Omitting directTruth because the prediction is temporally open.

claims.text: Angela Tinnell's research on virtual characters showed that mismatched emotional expressions in different regions of the face produce psychopath-like eeriness.

evidence.quote:
Angela Tinnell has led studies in virtual
characters

Turns out a happy smile and angry eyes is
not a good look

claims.rationale: Angela Tinwell (spelled 'Tinnell' by the host) is a UK psychology researcher with multiple published studies on uncanny virtual characters and emotion-mismatch. The summary matches her published findings on facial asymmetry and dampened upper-face response.

claims.text: Uncanny Valley is an evolved self-preservation response helping humans detect emotionally or physically ill individuals.

evidence.quote:
generally thought of as a self-preservation
response encoded in our
DNA through evolution

Uncanny Valley helps us perceive threats by
detecting insincere emotions

claims.rationale: The pathogen-avoidance / corpse-aversion evolutionary account is one popular hypothesis (MacDorman & Ishiguro) but remains contested and lacks decisive evidence. Competing accounts (perceptual-mismatch, categorical-boundary) are equally plausible.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] The evolutionary-threat account competes with the mind-perception account as the underlying mechanism — they are alternative, not complementary, explanations of the core trigger.
